title: Practice Set 7 — Memory Management and Tungsten
description: Master Spark’s memory internals: map workloads to the unified memory model, diagnose execution-storage eviction cascades, evaluate GC strategies per workload type, design off-heap memory policies, and produce platform-wide memory configuration standards.

datasets.description: 100-executor JVM metrics: heap regions, GC type/time, spill, off-heap, per-task memory, across 5 workload types. 19 executors have GC >15%.

tasks.title: Map NordGrid’s Memory Budget to Spark’s Memory Regions

description.context: NordGrid’s Gold aggregation pipeline runs on executors with 16 GB heap (spark.executor.memory=16g). The pipeline caches a 3 GB Silver DataFrame and then performs a shuffle-heavy groupBy that needs 5 GB of execution memory. The engineer must understand whether 16 GB is enough by mapping the workload to Spark’s memory regions.
description.task: Calculate how Spark divides the 16 GB heap into regions: JVM overhead, unified memory (execution + storage), and user memory. Then determine whether the 3 GB cache + 5 GB shuffle fit within the budget.
description.validationHint: Spark’s unified memory model: heap × (1 - overhead) = usable. Usable × spark.memory.fraction (0.6 default) = unified region. Unified is shared dynamically between execution and storage.

evidence.content:
Executor config:
  spark.executor.memory = 16 GB (16,384 MB)
  spark.memory.fraction = 0.6 (default)
  spark.memory.storageFraction = 0.5 (default, within unified region)
  JVM overhead: ~25% (spark.executor.memoryOverhead)
  Cores per executor: 4 (spark.executor.cores)

Workload:
  Cache: 3 GB Silver DataFrame (stored in storage region)
  Shuffle: groupBy aggregation needs 5 GB execution memory
  Concurrent tasks: 4 (one per core)

fields.label: Usable heap after JVM overhead (16 GB × 0.75)?
Unified memory region (usable heap × 0.6)?
fields.rationale: 12,288 × 0.6 = 7,372 MB (~7.2 GB). This is shared between execution and storage.

fields.label: Can the 3 GB cache + 5 GB shuffle fit in the 7.2 GB unified region?
options: Yes — 3 + 5 = 8 GB fits in 7.2 GB because Spark compresses cached data

No — 3 + 5 = 8 GB exceeds the 7.2 GB unified region. Spark’s unified model allows execution to evict storage when needed, so the shuffle will evict cached data to make room. The 3 GB cache will be partially evicted, causing re-reads from disk.
No — the pipeline will crash with OOM
Yes — execution and storage have separate budgets that don’t share
fields.correctAnswer: No — 3 + 5 = 8 GB exceeds the 7.2 GB unified region. Spark’s unified model allows execution to evict storage when needed, so the shuffle will evict cached data to make room. The 3 GB cache will be partially evicted, causing re-reads from disk.

fields.label: What is the per-task memory budget for the 5 GB shuffle (4 concurrent tasks)?
options: 5 GB per task — each task gets the full execution region

~1.8 GB per task — the unified region (7.2 GB) minus cached data (3 GB) = 4.2 GB execution available, divided by 4 concurrent tasks = ~1.05 GB per task. Even without the cache, 7.2 GB / 4 = 1.8 GB per task.
~3.6 GB per task — the execution half of unified divided by 4
16 GB per task — tasks don’t share memory
fields.correctAnswer: ~1.8 GB per task — the unified region (7.2 GB) minus cached data (3 GB) = 4.2 GB execution available, divided by 4 concurrent tasks = ~1.05 GB per task. Even without the cache, 7.2 GB / 4 = 1.8 GB per task.

description.context: NordGrid’s Silver enrichment pipeline caches a 4 GB reference DataFrame for 6 downstream Gold pipelines to read. During the 3rd Gold pipeline’s shuffle-heavy join, the cached DataFrame is partially evicted. The 4th, 5th, and 6th Gold pipelines must re-read 2 GB from disk, adding 90 seconds each.
description.task: Trace the eviction cascade through Spark’s unified memory model. Determine why the eviction happened, when it happened, and how to prevent it.
description.validationHint: In unified memory, execution can evict storage (but not vice versa). The eviction occurs when execution memory demand exceeds its half of the unified region and borrows from storage.

timeline.label: Memory timeline during Gold pipeline 3
timeline.content:
T=0s:   Unified region: 7.2 GB. Storage: 4 GB (cached ref). Execution: 0 GB.
T=5s:   Gold-3 starts shuffle join. Execution requests 4 GB.
T=6s:   Only 3.2 GB free in unified (7.2 - 4.0 cache). Execution needs 4 GB.
T=7s:   Execution evicts 0.8 GB of cached ref to make room. Cache: 3.2 GB. Execution: 4 GB.
T=30s:  Hash table grows. Execution requests 5.5 GB total.
T=31s:  Execution evicts another 1.2 GB cache. Cache: 2 GB. Execution: 5.2 GB.
T=120s: Gold-3 completes. Execution releases 5.2 GB. Cache remains at 2 GB (evicted data not restored).
T=121s: Gold-4 reads cached ref. 2 GB in cache, 2 GB must be re-read from disk. +90s.
T=211s: Gold-5 reads. Same: 2 GB from cache, 2 GB from disk. +90s.
T=301s: Gold-6 reads. Same. +90s.

impact.label: Total impact
impact.content:
Without eviction: 6 Gold pipelines read cache at 3s each = 18s total
With eviction: Gold 1-2 read cache (6s) + Gold 3 executes (120s) + Gold 4-6 re-read disk (270s) = 396s
Wasted time: 270s from 3 pipelines re-reading 2 GB each from disk
Root cause: execution memory evicted storage during Gold-3’s join

fields.label: Why does execution evict storage and not the other way around?
options: Spark randomly chooses which to evict

In unified memory, execution has priority over storage. Execution memory (shuffles, joins, sorts) is needed for active computation and cannot be spilled without causing OOM. Storage memory (caches) can be evicted because the data can be recomputed or re-read from disk.
Storage is always evicted first because it’s less important
The order depends on which was allocated first
fields.correctAnswer: In unified memory, execution has priority over storage. Execution memory (shuffles, joins, sorts) is needed for active computation and cannot be spilled without causing OOM. Storage memory (caches) can be evicted because the data can be recomputed or re-read from disk.

fields.label: Why isn’t the evicted cache data restored after Gold-3 completes?
options: Spark automatically restores evicted cache blocks

Evicted cache blocks are permanently removed from the storage region. Spark does not re-cache them automatically — the application would need to call .cache() and trigger a read again. This is by design: automatic re-caching could cause an eviction loop.
The cache is restored but it takes time
Gold-3 corrupted the cache
fields.correctAnswer: Evicted cache blocks are permanently removed from the storage region. Spark does not re-cache them automatically — the application would need to call .cache() and trigger a read again. This is by design: automatic re-caching could cause an eviction loop.

fields.label: How should NordGrid prevent this eviction cascade?
options: Increase spark.memory.storageFraction to give storage a larger guaranteed minimum
Increase executor memory from 16 GB to 32 GB so the unified region (14.4 GB) fits both the 4 GB cache and the 5.5 GB execution peak without contention
Reduce cores per executor from 4 to 2 so fewer concurrent tasks compete for execution memory

All three approaches reduce contention, but increasing executor memory is the most direct fix. Reducing cores gives more memory per task but halves parallelism. Increasing storageFraction protects cache but restricts execution.
fields.correctAnswer: All three approaches reduce contention, but increasing executor memory is the most direct fix. Reducing cores gives more memory per task but halves parallelism. Increasing storageFraction protects cache but restricts execution.

tasks.title: Evaluate GC Tuning Options for NordGrid’s Workloads

description.context: NordGrid’s executor metrics show 19 executors with GC time > 15% (severe). These are concentrated in ml_training (27 executors, avg GC 16%) and aggregation_gold (17 executors, avg GC 11%). The platform team evaluates three GC strategies: current G1GC with default tuning, G1GC with tuned parameters, and ZGC (low-pause collector).
description.task: Evaluate the three GC options against NordGrid’s workload profiles. ML training has long-lived objects (model weights). Gold aggregation has short-lived objects (hash tables rebuilt per partition). Each workload needs a different GC strategy.
description.validationHint: G1GC works well for mixed workloads. ZGC minimizes pause time but has higher throughput overhead. The key: ML training benefits from larger heap + G1GC tuning. Gold aggregation benefits from more partitions (smaller per-task data) rather than GC tuning.

evidence.content:
Option A: G1GC default (current)
  Pause target: 200ms
  ML training: 16% GC time (frequent full-GC from large old-gen)
  Gold agg: 11% GC time (frequent young-GC from hash table churn)
  Cost: $0 (no change)

Option B: G1GC tuned
  -XX:G1HeapRegionSize=16m (larger regions for ML’s big objects)
  -XX:InitiatingHeapOccupancyPercent=35 (trigger concurrent GC earlier)
  ML training: estimated 10% GC (earlier concurrent collection avoids full-GC)
  Gold agg: estimated 9% GC (marginal improvement)
  Cost: testing and validation time

Option C: ZGC
  Sub-millisecond pauses (no stop-the-world)
  ML training: estimated 2% GC pause (but 5-8% throughput overhead from concurrent GC barriers)
  Gold agg: estimated 1% GC pause (same throughput overhead)
  Cost: requires JDK 17+, some Spark versions have compatibility issues
  Net effect: lower pause times but tasks run 5-8% slower overall

fields.label: Which GC strategy should ML training executors use?
options: Option A (G1GC default) — 16% GC is acceptable for ML

Option B (G1GC tuned) — reducing ML GC from 16% to 10% saves 6% of compute time. G1GC tuning is low-risk and addresses the specific problem: large old-gen objects from model weights trigger full-GC. Earlier concurrent collection prevents this.
Option C (ZGC) — lowest pause times
No GC tuning — increase executor memory instead
fields.correctAnswer: Option B (G1GC tuned) — reducing ML GC from 16% to 10% saves 6% of compute time. G1GC tuning is low-risk and addresses the specific problem: large old-gen objects from model weights trigger full-GC. Earlier concurrent collection prevents this.

fields.label: Should Gold aggregation executors use the same GC strategy as ML?
options: Yes — one GC config for the whole cluster is simpler

No — Gold’s GC problem is different. Hash table churn creates short-lived objects (young-gen pressure), not old-gen pressure. The better fix for Gold is increasing shuffle partitions (smaller hash tables per task) rather than GC tuning. GC tuning gives marginal improvement (11% to 9%) while more partitions addresses the root cause.
No — Gold should use ZGC while ML uses G1GC
Yes — G1GC tuned helps all workloads equally
fields.correctAnswer: No — Gold’s GC problem is different. Hash table churn creates short-lived objects (young-gen pressure), not old-gen pressure. The better fix for Gold is increasing shuffle partitions (smaller hash tables per task) rather than GC tuning. GC tuning gives marginal improvement (11% to 9%) while more partitions addresses the root cause.

fields.label: When should NordGrid consider ZGC (Option C)?
options: Never — G1GC is always sufficient

For latency-sensitive streaming pipelines where pause time directly affects micro-batch duration. ZGC’s sub-ms pauses prevent the 200ms G1GC pauses that can cause micro-batch deadline misses. The 5-8% throughput overhead is acceptable when latency SLA is the priority.
For all workloads once JDK 17 is adopted
Only for ML training
fields.correctAnswer: For latency-sensitive streaming pipelines where pause time directly affects micro-batch duration. ZGC’s sub-ms pauses prevent the 200ms G1GC pauses that can cause micro-batch deadline misses. The 5-8% throughput overhead is acceptable when latency SLA is the priority.

description.context: NordGrid’s platform team evaluates off-heap memory (spark.memory.offHeap.enabled) for workloads where GC pressure is the bottleneck. Off-heap memory is allocated outside the JVM heap, so it doesn’t contribute to GC pressure. But it adds operational complexity: memory must be managed manually, and debugging is harder.
description.task: Determine which NordGrid workloads benefit from off-heap memory and which do not. Design the off-heap policy: which pipeline types get off-heap, how much to allocate, and what to monitor.
description.validationHint: Off-heap helps when: (1) GC is the bottleneck (>15% GC time), (2) the workload has large serialized data structures (shuffles, broadcast tables), (3) the workload is long-running (streaming state). Off-heap doesn’t help when GC is already low or when UDFs allocate on-heap objects.

evidence.content:
Workload          | Executors | Avg GC% | Spill Rate | Primary Memory Consumer     | Off-Heap Benefit?
ml_training       | 27        | 16%     | High       | Model weights (on-heap)     | Low — model objects are Java objects, must be on-heap
aggregation_gold  | 17        | 11%     | Medium     | Hash tables (Tungsten)      | Medium — Tungsten already uses binary encoding
join_enrichment   | 20        | 7%      | Low        | Broadcast tables + shuffle  | High — broadcast + shuffle buffers benefit from off-heap
streaming_ingest  | 26        | 5%      | None       | Streaming state             | High — state stored off-heap reduces GC for long-running jobs
etl_silver        | 10        | 6%      | Low        | Scan buffers                | Low — GC is already low, no bottleneck to fix

fields.label: Which workloads should use off-heap memory?
options: All workloads — off-heap is always better

streaming_ingest and join_enrichment. Streaming benefits because state stored off-heap doesn’t trigger GC for 24/7 jobs. Join enrichment benefits because broadcast tables and shuffle buffers are serialized data that Spark can store off-heap efficiently.
Only ml_training — it has the highest GC%
None — off-heap adds too much complexity
fields.correctAnswer: streaming_ingest and join_enrichment. Streaming benefits because state stored off-heap doesn’t trigger GC for 24/7 jobs. Join enrichment benefits because broadcast tables and shuffle buffers are serialized data that Spark can store off-heap efficiently.
fields.distractorRationale: ML training has the highest GC% but its memory consumers (model weights) are Java objects that MUST live on-heap. Off-heap only helps serialized binary data (Tungsten-encoded). ETL has low GC, so no bottleneck to fix.

fields.label: Why doesn’t off-heap help ML training despite its 16% GC?
options: Off-heap is incompatible with ML libraries

ML model weights are Java/Scala objects (arrays, matrices) that the JVM must manage on-heap. Off-heap stores serialized binary data. Since model objects can’t be serialized into off-heap during active computation, they remain on-heap and continue to cause GC pressure. The correct fix for ML is G1GC tuning (Task 3) or larger heap.
ML training doesn’t use enough memory for off-heap to matter
Off-heap is only for streaming workloads
fields.correctAnswer: ML model weights are Java/Scala objects (arrays, matrices) that the JVM must manage on-heap. Off-heap stores serialized binary data. Since model objects can’t be serialized into off-heap during active computation, they remain on-heap and continue to cause GC pressure. The correct fix for ML is G1GC tuning (Task 3) or larger heap.

fields.label: How much off-heap memory should streaming executors allocate?
options: Same as heap — double the total memory

Equal to the expected state size. For streaming_ingest with ~2-4 GB state, allocate 4 GB off-heap (spark.memory.offHeap.size=4g). This moves state out of the heap, reducing GC from 5% to ~2% without over-provisioning.
1 GB — minimal off-heap is sufficient
Off-heap size doesn’t matter — Spark manages it automatically
fields.correctAnswer: Equal to the expected state size. For streaming_ingest with ~2-4 GB state, allocate 4 GB off-heap (spark.memory.offHeap.size=4g). This moves state out of the heap, reducing GC from 5% to ~2% without over-provisioning.

description.context: NordGrid needs platform-wide memory configuration standards. Currently, every team sets executor memory ad-hoc, resulting in 19 executors with severe GC (>15%), 74 with unnecessary spill, and inconsistent off-heap usage. The platform team must produce a standards document that specifies memory configuration by workload type.
description.task: Produce a memory configuration standards document as a DataFrame. Each row specifies the recommended configuration for one workload type: heap size, memory fraction, GC type, off-heap settings, cores-per-executor, and monitoring thresholds.
description.validationHint: Different workloads need different configs: ML needs large heap + G1GC tuning. Streaming needs off-heap for state. Gold aggregation needs more partitions rather than more memory. ETL needs minimal config. One-size-fits-all is the anti-pattern.

evidence.content:
Current state:
  19 executors with GC > 15% (mostly ml_training)
  74 executors spilling (insufficient execution memory per task)
  23 executors using off-heap (ad-hoc, no standard)
  Heap sizes: 25 at 8 GB, 37 at 16 GB, 38 at 32 GB (inconsistent)

The standards must specify per workload type:
  1. Recommended heap size
  2. spark.memory.fraction (default 0.6 or custom)
  3. GC type and tuning flags
  4. Off-heap: enabled/disabled, size
  5. Cores per executor
  6. Monitoring: GC% threshold for alerting

fields.label: Why is a single memory config for all workloads wrong?
options: It’s not wrong — standardization requires one config

Different workloads have different memory profiles: ML needs large heap for model objects (on-heap). Streaming needs off-heap for state. Aggregation needs more partitions, not more memory. One config either wastes resources (over-provisioned for ETL) or causes failures (under-provisioned for ML).
Different workloads only differ in CPU, not memory
One config works if the heap is large enough
fields.correctAnswer: Different workloads have different memory profiles: ML needs large heap for model objects (on-heap). Streaming needs off-heap for state. Aggregation needs more partitions, not more memory. One config either wastes resources (over-provisioned for ETL) or causes failures (under-provisioned for ML).

fields.label: What GC% threshold should trigger an alert?
options: Any GC above 0% is a problem

GC > 10% for batch workloads, GC > 5% for streaming. Batch can tolerate moderate GC because pauses don’t affect SLA (the job just takes slightly longer). Streaming’s micro-batch deadlines are sensitive to pauses, so the threshold is tighter.
GC > 50% — only alert on extreme cases
Same threshold (15%) for all workloads
fields.correctAnswer: GC > 10% for batch workloads, GC > 5% for streaming. Batch can tolerate moderate GC because pauses don’t affect SLA (the job just takes slightly longer). Streaming’s micro-batch deadlines are sensitive to pauses, so the threshold is tighter.

starterScaffold.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, lit

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName('nordgrid-memory-standards').master('local[*]').getOrCreate()

df_metrics = spark.read.csv('PX7_data_executor_jvm_metrics.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)

# YOUR SOLUTION BELOW
# Produce memory configuration standards as DataFrame.
# Each row = one workload type’s recommended config.
# Required columns: workload_type, heap_gb, memory_fraction,
#   gc_type, gc_tuning_flags, off_heap_enabled, off_heap_gb,
#   cores_per_executor, gc_alert_threshold_pct, rationale
# 5 workload types: etl_silver, aggregation_gold, ml_training,
#   join_enrichment, streaming_ingest
# Store in 'memory_standards'



# YOUR SOLUTION ABOVE

# Verification
print('Memory Configuration Standards:')
memory_standards.show(truncate=False)
print(f'Workload types covered: {memory_standards.count()}')

assertions.check: all(c in memory_standards.columns for c in ['workload_type', 'heap_gb', 'gc_type', 'off_heap_enabled', 'cores_per_executor'])

assertions.description: Different GC configs for different workloads

conceptsCombined: Unified memory model: execution vs storage regions (Task 1, Lessons 1-2)
Execution-storage contention and eviction cascade (Task 2, Lessons 3-4)
GC tuning per workload type: G1GC vs ZGC (Task 3, Lessons 5-6)
Off-heap memory: when it helps vs when it doesn’t (Task 4, Lessons 7-8)
Monitoring thresholds differentiated by workload sensitivity (PM7)
synthesisNotes.rationale: This capstone produces a 5-row standards document where every row is a different memory architecture: ML (large heap, G1GC tuned, no off-heap), streaming (moderate heap, off-heap for state, low GC threshold), aggregation (moderate heap, more partitions not more memory), etc. The anti-pattern is one-size-fits-all. The student must match each workload’s memory profile to the right combination of heap, GC, off-heap, and monitoring.

selfCheck.scaleMatters: 100 executors across 5 workload types need different configs. Single-executor tuning is mid-level.
selfCheck.theoryPracticeBoundary: Theory teaches memory model and GC concepts. Practice requires diagnosing cross-region contention and designing per-workload standards that prevent the 19 severe-GC executors.
